headlines.title: Own Decree Makes President Huerta Mexican Dictator
JURY COMPLETED TO TRY GEORGE ON SWINDLING CHARGE
headlines.subtitle: Taking of Evidence in First of Reservoir Cases Begins, DEFENSE OUTLINED Attorneys Will Contend Defendant Never Got a Cent Wrongfully. TERRELL AIDING STATE Jurors Asked if City's Suit Against Concern Would Affect Opinions.
headlines.fullText:
A Jury to try W. J. George, superintendent of the Underground Construction Company, charged with conspiracy to swindle the city, was selected in Judge Swayne's court Tuesday morning. Taking of testimony began Tuesday afternoon.
The jurors trying the case are: La W. Carpenter, city: J. IL. Reed, Arlington; J. F. Bussey Arlington; G. N. Potter, Haslett: E. B. Roui, Handley: J. 5. Moore, Bedford: T. P. Hunky WL Toask, Riverside; JL Busby, Kennedale: J. C. Pilant, Arlington: 1. W. Anderson, Euless, and 2. Powell, Riverside.
The Busbys are brothers, one a school teacher and the other a farmer.
"Will you be like most brothers and disagree" they were asked by Attorney Mclean when the panel was questioned. of the Jury.
Potter have had some work Potter now farming, formerly had some hauling on contract work W. 1. Trask was formerly a teamster. Both are farmers.
Never Got a Dollar-Defense.
The contention of the defense would be that George never got a dollar wrongfully from the city and that the Underground Construction Company did not and that he and officials of the company never conspired to get It. At another time, he said a point in issue would be whether the city was trying to beat these people of these people trying to lieat the city. Ile asked if this suit might affect the suit in the federal court.
At another time he asked a prospective furor as to his acquaintance with members of the city and "enmity' grew up between them. He asserted also that they were going to show that the city wouldn't give the contractors what they were entitled to and that they tried to get what they' were entitled to.
Other jurors were questioned as to any opinion formed as a result of the part played by the reservoir in the last city campaign.
Terrell Aids in Case.
It also developed during the questioning that Former County Judge John L. Terrell Is preparing evidence in the case for the prosecution. This admission was made when Mclean began questioning as to acquaintances among the county attorney's force and O. W. Gillespie, who was employed by the city commission to aid the prosecution, he asked Gillespie if Terrell were in the case. Gillespie said he would assist In getting up the evidence. He then stated that Terrell was a member of the firm of which he was a member, but was not employed by the city so far as the records show.
Judge Swayne ruled lite Monday afternoon that the indictment against George and McCormick is good. He then granted a motion for a severance of the trial of McCormick and George. McCormick was not in court Tuesday morning.
Other members of the jury panel were excused by Judge Swayne until Thursday morning af 10 o'clock. In sending them home he warned them not to stay in town and tell folks he did not excuse them.
"The papers are going to publish that you were excused," he said. "so you better go home and chop the wood."

headlines.title: COMMERCE BODIES DECLARE APPROVAL OF CURRENCY BILL
headlines.subtitle: Referendum Vote Taken Throughout U. S. OPPOSITION SLIGHT 'Measure Embodies Elements Necessary for Safe Banking System.' SEVERAL CHANGES URGED Nine Members Instead of Seven Favored for Federal Reserve Board.
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
DETROIT, Oct. 14 .- The constituent membership of the Chamber of Commerce of the United States of America, including local chambers of commerce. national trade: organisations in all parts of the country, has approved by a referendum vote the report of its banking and currency committee on the pending Owens-Glass currency bill.
The board of directors of the chamber in session here, completed the canvass of the vote today. It expressed the opinion that there was no doubt that the vote reflected the sentiment of the business organizations strongly, in favor of the measure. The vote was 304 for and 17 against.
The committee in its report, which was made the basis of the referendum. stated:
"We regard the measure as a piece of constructive legislation and believe that it embodies in a large degree elements necessary to provide the nation with a safe currency and banking system."
Seven Recommendations.
Seven recommendations for amendment of the measure were submitted for a vote. All were approved by large separate majorities
The separate recommendations were:
1. In favor of the increase of the federal reserve board to nine members, the two additional members to be chosen by the original members.
2. Creation of the Federal advisory council to be elected by the regional reserve banks, the president and vice president of the council to reside In Washington and to sit at meetings of the federal reserve board, their salaries to be fixed by the board itself.
3. That in the creation of the new reserve districts a beginning be made with the present central reserve cities (three in number).

headlines.title: 400 WELSH MINERS TRAPPED BY FIRE AND EXPLOSION DIE
headlines.subtitle: Rescuers Driven From Shaft by Bad Air. FLAMES ARE FATAL Intake Airway Throttled by Fire After Coal Workers Are Entombed. DEATH TRAP ONCE BEFORE All Perished in Early Disaster. Few Survivors Brought to Surface.
headlines.fullText:
By associated Press.
CARDIFF, Wales, Oct. 14 .- More than 400 Welsh coal miners perished, It Is believed, from fire and after-damp here today. A terrifle explosion shattered the works shortly after 931 men had descended into the pit.
At first It was stated the day shift numbered 700 men, but later figures showed 931 men had gone down.
The rescued miners and members of the life-saving gangs, too, declared they could hold out no hope for those of the workmen still below.
Fire Adds to Horror.
The men brought to the surface were found on the east side of the mine, where the ventilation remained fairly good, On the west side where the explosion occurred, fire soon added its terrors and the rescue parties were unable to penetrate far. The rescuers were required to keep back the crowds of women and children obtaining news of kinsmen. Most of the rescued men were suffering from burns, shock or effects of poisonous gases.
The day shift of 931 men descended the shafts in the cages at 6 o'clock. An hour afterward a deafening report brought people running to the pit head.
Explosion is Disastrous.
They found the ventilating and hoisting machinery at the top of the main shaft had been decapitated by the blast.
Rescue parties belonging to the mine at once made preparations to enter the mine in an endeavor to save the entombed men.
An entrance was found by way of an adjacent shaft.

headlines.title: Huerta's Proclamation Making Himself Dictator
headlines.fullText:
Huerta's proclamation, dated Oct, 10, follows:
"In view of the fact that the chamber of deputies and senators have been dissolved and inhabilitated to perform their functions, I have seen fit to decree that Article 1, the constitutional exemption from arrest and judicial action, which the citizens who form the Twenty-sixth congress enjoyed in view of their functions, is hereby repealed."
In a decree under date of Oct. 10, Huerta declares:
Until the people elect new magistrates to take over legislative powers, and in order to re-establish constitutional order in the shortest possible time. Victoriano Huerta, constitutional president ad interim, has seen fit to decree these articles:
"Article 1-The judicial power of the federation shall continue in its functions within the limits set by the constitution of the republic and decree of the executive of Oct. 10, and such others as shall be issued by him.
"Article 2-The executive conserves the powers conferred upon him by the constitution in the departments of gobernacion, finance and war only for the time absolutely necessary for the re-establishment of legislative power. In the meantime the executive takes upon himself the aforesaid mentioned powers and will make use of them by issuing degrees which shall be observed generally and which he may deem expedient for the public welfare.
"Article 3- Executive of the union will render an account to the legislative power of the use which he makes of the powers which he assumes by means of this decree as Soon as this is in function,
(Signed) "VICTORIANO HUERTA."

headlines.title: HUERTA DECLARES HIMSELF DICTATOR IN PROCLAMATION
headlines.subtitle: President Suspends Part of Constitution. TAKES AWAY RIGHTS Mexican Congressmen Not Further Exempt From Arrest, Asserts Ruler. DISCUSSED BY U. S. CABINET Reply to Wilson's Warning Now in Preparation-Displeasure Indicated.
headlines.fullText:
Serial lo The Star-Telepress,
WASHINGTON, oct. 14 .- President Huerta has proclaimed himself dictator of Mexico and has lost no time in assuming powers of Congress, In his proclamation, copies of which were received by the United states cabinet today, Huerta suspends that part of the constitution that makes congressmen immune from arrest.
That the Mexican administration looks with displeasure on the warning sent by President Wilson against harming any of the officials arrested by Huerta was indicated today by advices from the City of Mexico.
The cabinet in session there is working on a reply to the United States and It is believed will show resentment at the interference.
That the situation is regarded as more tense than since the killing of Madero is shown by the quick positioning of foreign warships with a view to protect American and foreign interests. The dispatch of a German warship to Mexico is taken as a sign that other countries will do likewise.
Will Reply to United States.
CITY OF MEXICO, Oct. 14 .- An early answer will be made by the Mexican government to the note from Washington stating that the United States would look with displeasure upon any injury to the Mexican deputies under arrest, according to the Mexican foreign minister, Querido Moheno. It was the subject of a cabinet meeting which lasted until an early hour this morning.
Mexico's reply probably will be delivered late today to Nelson O'Shaughnessy, the American charge d'affaires.
Senor Moheno described the terms of the note, but Senor Moheno described it as intemperate. On receipt of the communication, Moheno admitted saying: "If with this motion in view the relations with the American charge d'affaires has been broken, it will be because of the Intemperate language of his government."
A further conference of ministers was held today to discuss the nature of the Mexican reply.
Cabinet Discusses Situation.
WASHINGTON. Oct. 16 .- President Wilson and his entire cabinet discussed the Mexican situation and the naval policy of the United States today at the first cabinet meeting since last June.
While the first object of the cabinet meeting had been to take up a three-year naval program, the presence of United States warships in Mexican waters and the possible action of other powers which may foresee a crisis for the Huerta: government, commanded first attention, Advices from the City of Mexico indicated that the Mexican deputies, for whose safety the American government had made representations, still were in Jail, but, according to Huerta, in no danger.
His statement. however, that the deputies would be brought to trial was received here with disappointment. It is believed that foreign governments are also watching the fate of the imprisoned congressmen.

headlines.title: GRAIN DEALERS TO CONSIDER ACTION ON ANTI-FUTURES
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
NEW ORLEANS, Det. 14-The several hundred delegates from every section of the country to the annual convention of the National Grain Dealers' association were called to order this morning by President Charles D. Jones of Nashville Tenn. After the addresses of welcome and the responses by L. K. Ellsworth, president of the Ohio association, were furnished, Jones delivered the annual address. The morning's business ended with the report of the secretary-treasurer and appointment of committees.
The delegates are particularly interested in the question of uniform system of grading. As each delegate arrives he is taken before an executive session of the legislative committee, where he gives his views of three important subjects. The report of this committee, embodying its recommendations, is awaited with much interest by the delegates from the Northwest, who will arrive on a special train bringing the Chicago and Northwestern representations.

headlines.title: PROSECUTION MOVES TO BROADEN SCOPE OF SULZER CHARGES
headlines.fullText:
ALBANY, N. Y. Oct. 14-A motion to amend Article IV of the Impeachment charges against Governor Sulzer, so as to include the testimony of Ambassador Morgenthau and Superintendent of Public Works Duncan W. Peck, was presented by the impeachment managers at the reopening of the trial today.
No action was suggested in regard to the testimony of Allan A. Ryan. that William Sulzer wilfully attempted to incite and procure F. L. Colwell, Louis A. Sarecky, Melville D. Fuller, Duncan W. Peck and Henry Morgenthau to commit perjury and to give false testimony before the Frawley Investigating committee.
Article IV, as it now stands does not mention either Peck or Morgenthau, and merely sets forth that Sulzer "practiced deceit and, fraud and used threats and menaces with intent to prevent said committee from procuring the attendance and testimony of Sarecky, Colwell and Fuller. This proposed amendment was opposed by Judge D. Cady Herrick for the defense.
"I do not mean." Stanchfield said, "to argue here that this tribunal possesses the power to frame new charges in such a way as to be necessary to contort or twist this tribunal into an impeachment body. I concede with the utmost frankness that the assembly alone is the Impeaching power of this state, This by no means precludes this court from amending the impeachment so long as the amendment is not unjust to the respondent. If the respondent feels that he needs more time to meet the new stand or if he feels now, in the light of the testimony he desires to produce to meet the accusations, the door is open and there is no objection raised by the managers of this trial"
Attorney Stanchfield emphasized the fact that Article IV, as It now stands, charges Sulzer with the suppression of witnesses, while the testimony of Peck and Morgenthau showed he was guilty of attempted subornation of perjury, a felony.
There seemed little probability, it was said, that the assembly would attempt to bring a new charge covering the Ryan testimony at this late date. This testimony was that Governor Sulzer had besought him to obtain the influence of William Barnes to stop the trial. According to the impeachment managers, this constitutes bribery, but it could not be fitted into Article IV or any of the other articles except as a general corroborative proposition that the governor is unfit to hold office.

headlines.title: Man Acquitted of Killing Deputy.
headlines.fullText: SAN MARCOS, Texas, Oct., 14. - Jesse Barres, on trial since Friday on a charge of complicity with Sango Parro, who received the death penalty in June, for killing Deputy Sheriff John Davis was acquitted by the jury last night. Parro will not be tried until the next term of court.

headlines.title: Oil Pipe Line Case Opens.
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON, Oct. 14 .- The case of the Standard oil and other oil companies to prevent the interstate commerce commission from assuming jurisdiction of oil pipe lines was the first case argued before the supreme court today. The constitutionality of the "pipe line" amendment of 1906 to the interstate commerce law is involved.

headlines.title: REPORTS OF EASTERN STAR OFFICERS READ
headlines.subtitle: Officers Will Be Elected at Dallas Session Thursday afternoon.
headlines.fullText:
DALLAS, Oct. 14. Reports of grand officers was the feature of the annual meeting of the grand chapter of Texas, order of the Eastern Star, which opened here with a reception to the 200 delegates last night at the Scottish Rite Cathedral tonight.
The afternoon session today was to be devoted to addresses with a musical program.
Election of officers will not take place until Thursday.

headlines.title: Unborn Child of Accident Victim Awarded Damages
headlines.fullText:
An unborn child won a-judgment in court today when Judge Marvin Brown made an order carrying out an agreement in the suit of Mrs. Lela Riley Mccarthy et al against the Fort Worth Belt Railway Company.
Mrs. Mccarthy's husband was killed in an accident on the railway. Judgment was given Mrs. Mccarthy in the sum of $5,500. It was ordered $500 go to the posthumous child of the deceased if there should be such a child, and the remaining $5,000 to Mrs. Mccarthy. Mrs. Mccarthy's attorney, was given $250.

headlines.title: $30,000 Loss in Dallas Fire.
headlines.fullText: DALLAS, Oct. 14- Thirty thousand dollars was the estimate of damage resulting from a fire early this morning in a business block occupied by a number of firms at Commerce and Field streets.

headlines.title: CONGRESSMAN HENRY SPEAKS AT TYLER FAIR
headlines.subtitle: Parade Precedes Program and Races Held in Afternoon-Stores Close.
headlines.fullText:
TYLER Texas Oct. 14.-The East Texas fair opened today with a large crowd. Congressman Henry delivered the chief address. His talk was principally devoted to the resources of Texas and the opportunities in this state. C. W. Boon, president of the association; Mayor B. B. Cain and others also spoke.
Exhibits consist of agricultural, horticultural, culinary and livestock.
In the afternoon races were held and last night there was a band concert. Yesterday was Tyler Day and every downtown store closed at noon to permit employees to enjoy the fair. A parade, headed by the city and fair officials, preceded the opening of the program yesterday.
